meta.title: Farewell Address at Springfield, Illinois
meta.authorship_notes: Two versions exist: extemporaneous transcription and Lincoln's written reconstruction. Text below is the self-edited written version Lincoln approved.

document_summary.primary_finding: The Springfield Farewell is the earliest formal public address in the corpus and the first document to activate cluster_06 (Providence) in a sustained way. The three-sentence Providence arc (ext_009) introduces the framing that will dominate the Second Inaugural: God attended Washington during his task; God must attend Lincoln during his greater task; trust in God's omnipresence is the only basis for confidence. The key diachronic observation: at this point (February 1861) the Providence framing is entirely personal — God is Lincoln's companion and protector, not the war's judge and accountant. The theodicy logic (God as divine accountant extracting payment for sin) has not yet developed. The document activates cluster_05 (Washington as founding-father predecessor) and cluster_06 (Providence as attendant) but clusters 01, 02, 03, and 04 are absent. This is the most stripped formal public address in the corpus — nine sentences, no wound, no covenant, no proposition, no birth.
document_summary.annotator_notes: Four instances across three sentences of substantive content. The register is formal_public_address but the occasion is personal departure — Lincoln is speaking to his neighbors, not to the nation. This hybrid occasion (personal address to a community before a national task) explains the mixture of clusters: cluster_05 (the Washington comparison establishes national-historical framing) and cluster_06 (the Providence framing is both personal and national). risk_flag two_versions: text is Lincoln's self-edited written version, highest-confidence version of the two.

sentences.text: My friends, no one, not in my situation, can appreciate my feeling of sadness at this parting.
To this place, and the kindness of these people, I owe everything.
Here I have lived a quarter of a century, and have passed from a young to an old man.
Here my children have been born, and one is buried.
I now leave, not knowing when, or whether ever, I may return, with a task before me greater than that which rested upon Washington.

metaphor_instances.linguistic_form: a task before me greater than that which rested upon Washington
metaphor_instances.source_domain: ancestral inheritance — Washington as founding predecessor whose task Lincoln inherits and exceeds
metaphor_instances.target_domain: Lincoln's presidential mission measured against Washington's Revolutionary mission

mapping.task...rested upon Washington: Washington's mission as a physical burden inherited from history
mapping.greater than: Lincoln's task exceeds the founding precedent in difficulty
mapping.rested upon: the burden as something that presses down on the shoulders — bodily weight metaphor

entailments: Washington's task was the founding generation's defining burden
Lincoln inherits a burden from the same lineage — the same national mission
The comparison establishes the Civil War as the greatest crisis in the founding lineage

koenigsberg.obligatory_frame: If Lincoln's task exceeds Washington's, then the obligation is proportionally greater — what Washington's generation accomplished through revolution, Lincoln must accomplish through civil war. The comparison makes the burden inescapable.
koenigsberg.annotator_notes: 'Rested upon' carries a faint bodily resonance — the task as physical weight on Washington's body/shoulders. This creates minor cluster_01 co-activation (body bearing a burden) but cluster_05 is primary. The self-deprecating framing ('greater than that which rested upon Washington') is rhetorically significant: Lincoln is positioning himself not as Washington's equal but as the inheritor of a harder version of the same task. This is cluster_05's obligatory frame: you cannot refuse an inherited task greater than what the founders bore.

sentences.text: Without the assistance of the Divine Being who ever attended him, I cannot succeed.

metaphor_instances.linguistic_form: Divine Being who ever attended him
metaphor_instances.source_domain: a divine attendant or companion — Providence as personal guardian
metaphor_instances.target_domain: God's presence accompanying Washington through the Revolutionary crisis

mapping.Divine Being: God as active participant in historical events
mapping.attended him: Providence as companion — accompanying, not directing
mapping.without...I cannot succeed: divine assistance is a necessary condition for Lincoln's success

entailments: God was causally present during Washington's founding mission
The same Providence is required for Lincoln's mission
Without divine accompaniment, human effort is insufficient

koenigsberg.obligatory_frame: If God attended Washington and must attend Lincoln, then Lincoln's mission is divinely sanctioned — refusal or failure is not merely political defeat but theological abandonment.
koenigsberg.annotator_notes: This is the earliest instance of cluster_06 in the corpus (Phase 1, pre-war). At this stage, Providence is an attendant companion — God 'attended' Washington like a guardian spirit. This is a pre-punitive Providence: God accompanies the righteous, not yet God extracting blood-payment from both sides. The lincoln_non_agent flag is appropriate: Lincoln cannot succeed without God, framing his agency as conditional on divine assistance.

sentences.text: With that assistance I cannot fail.

metaphor_instances.linguistic_form: With that assistance I cannot fail
metaphor_instances.source_domain: divine guarantee — God's assistance as a condition that makes failure impossible
metaphor_instances.target_domain: providential assurance that the Union's cause will succeed

mapping.with that assistance: God's active participation as sufficient cause

entailments: God's assistance converts uncertain human effort into guaranteed success
The framing is conditional: failure = absence of divine assistance; success = its presence
Lincoln claims neither credit nor accountability — both belong to Providence

koenigsberg.obligatory_frame: The strong conditional claim ('cannot fail') converts Providence from a request into a guarantee — if God is with him, the cause is already won. This makes the war feel theologically predetermined.
koenigsberg.annotator_notes: The symmetry of s06/s07 is structurally important: 'Without...I cannot succeed / With that assistance I cannot fail.' The chiastic structure creates logical closure — success and failure are both conditionally assigned to Providence. Lincoln is neither the agent of success nor the cause of failure. This is the earliest and most concise form of the non-agent framing that becomes central in the Hodges Letter and Second Inaugural.

sentences.text: Trusting in Him who can go with me, and remain with you, and be everywhere for good, let us confidently hope that all will yet be well.

metaphor_instances.linguistic_form: Him who can go with me, and remain with you, and be everywhere for good
metaphor_instances.source_domain: an omnipresent personal companion — God as a being who can be simultaneously present in multiple places
metaphor_instances.target_domain: Providence as the source of shared hope for both Lincoln and the Springfield community

mapping.go with me: God accompanies Lincoln to Washington — a traveling companion
mapping.remain with you: God stays with the Springfield community simultaneously
mapping.be everywhere for good: divine omnipresence as the guarantor that separation does not mean abandonment

entailments: God is not limited by geography — Lincoln's departure does not remove divine presence from Springfield
The community's prayers and Lincoln's mission are united through God's omnipresence
Trust in omnipresent Providence is the basis for hoping 'all will yet be well'

koenigsberg.obligatory_frame: God's omnipresence makes Lincoln's departure theologically safe — the community is not abandoned, and the mission is divinely supervised from start to finish.
koenigsberg.annotator_notes: The phrase 'be everywhere for good' is the most expansive claim in the address: God is not just attending Lincoln (as in Washington's case) but is literally everywhere for the purpose of good. This is omnipresence put in service of the war's moral purpose. It converts the farewell's personal sadness into a theological confidence: the parting is safe because the same God will be on both sides of the separation.

sentences.text: To His care commending you, as I hope in your prayers you will commend me, I bid you an affectionate farewell.
